content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
August24, 1998
Michelle Knight
Adventures By The Sea
299 Cannery Row
Monterey, California 93940
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-183
Dear Ms. Knight:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”),’ as they relate to your ability to lawfully renegotiate/renew a
concession agreement with the City ofPacific Grove, while you are a candidate for elective
office. Our advice is limited to the provisions of the Act. We cannot provide you with advice
regarding any other laws that may be applicable to you.
We are unable to respond to your request for advice regarding the future renewal of a
lease agreement that you may have with the City ofPacific Grove, as your letter fails to contain
sufficient facts in order for us to conduct an analysis, and because, as a candidate, you presently
have no duties as a public official which are now in question regarding the lease.
We will only
provide formal written advice to a person whose duties under the Act are in question, and only
when all facts material to consideration of the question have been provided in a clear and concise
manner. (Regulation 18329(b).) We are therefore treating your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2 Enclosed with this letter, however, are some
informational materials that you may find helpful in analyzing the issue for yourself.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; and Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).)


File No. 1-98-183
Page 2
QUESTION
Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit you from renegotiating/renewing
your concession agreement with the City ofPacific Grove, while you are a candidate for the
Pacific Grove City Council?
CONCLUSION
As you are not a public official, the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act would not
prohibit you from renegotiating/renewing your concession agreement with the City of Pacific
Grove, while you are a candidate for the Pacific Grove City Council.
FACTS
You are a candidate for election to the Pacific Grove City Council, in the November 3,
1998 election. You and your husband own a business that presently has a concession agreement
with the City ofPacific Grove. The agreement is up for renegotiation/renewal on January 1,
1999. Tn order to prevent the renegotiation/renewal from occurring at a time when you may be a
member of the city council, the city has agreed to undertalce the renegotiation/renewal process
prior to the upcoming election. Ifthe agreement is renewed, it would begin immediately, and
supersede the previous agreement. Thereafier, if you are elected to the city council, and any
issues arise regarding the city’s relationship with your concession business while you are holding
office, you will remove yourself from any discussions and/or decisions by the city regarding your
business.
ANALYSIS
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act are found at Section 87100 et seq. Section
87100 provides that:
“No public official at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in malcing or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence
a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.” [emphasis added.]
The term “public official” is defined by Section 82048 of the Act, as follows:
“Public official’ means every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
or local government agency, but does not include judges and court commissioners
in the judicial branch of government.”
According to the definition of “public official” contained in Section 82048 of the Act, a
mere candidate for city council is not considered to be a public official. As such, the conflict-of
interest provisions of the Act do not apply to you as a candidate, and they do not affect your
ability to renegotiate/renew your concession agreement with the city, while you are a candidate.
Ifyou are elected to the city council, you should certainly feel free to write to us for
further advice regarding potential conflicts of interest. As noted earlier, however, we can only


File No. 1-98-183
Page 3
provide advice regarding the provisions of the Act. We cannot offer advice regarding any other
laws that may be applicable to you as a public official, such as Government Code Section 1090,
or the doctrine of incompatible offices.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Steven Benito Russo
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Grove, while you are a candidate for the Pacific Grove City Council.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit you from renegotiating/renewing
your concession agreement with the City ofPacific Grove, while you are a candidate for the
Pacific Grove City Council?

CONCLUSION: As you are not a public official, the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act would not
prohibit you from renegotiating/renewing your concession agreement with the City of Pacific
Grove, while you are a candidate for the Pacific Grove City Council.
